case_name: Monnet Ispat & Energy Ltd vs C.C.E., Raipur
court: Customs Excise & Service Tax Appellate Tribunal, Court-IV, New Delhi
facts: Monnet Ispat & Energy Ltd (appellant), a manufacturer of sponge iron and other iron and steel products, was issued a show cause notice seeking denial of service tax (Cenvat) credit totalling Rs.4,36,650. The Assistant Commissioner allowed part of the credit but disallowed Rs.38,510 relating to marine inland transit insurance for captive power plant machinery, money-in-transit insurance, personnel accident insurance for factory workers, follow-up services for installation and certain telephone services; a penalty of Rs.25,000 was also imposed. The Commissioner (Appeals) upheld the denial and penalty, and the appellant appealed to the CESTAT.
arguments_claimant: The appellant argued that transit insurance for plant and machinery, money-in-transit insurance, personnel accident insurance, follow-up services for installation and relevant telephone services constitute 'input services' and therefore qualify for Cenvat credit under the definition of input service; inward transportation of inputs/capital goods covers transit insurance. Counsel relied on Tribunal precedents (C.C.E., Mumbai v. GTC Industries Ltd. (Tri-LB), Finolex Cables Ltd., Millipore India Ltd.) and Board Circular No.665/56/2002-CX to show cenvat credit for capital goods and related services used in manufacturing even when electricity is for captive use.
arguments_respondent: The Revenue contended that the services must be used in the factory in relation to manufacture of finished products to qualify; marine inland transit insurance, money-in-transit insurance, follow-up services and the telephone services at the GM's residence and guest house were not shown to be in relation to manufacture. The Department further argued that accident insurance related to contract workers supplied by contractors and not to the appellant's own employees, so cenvat credit should not be allowed.
ratio_decidendi: The Tribunal held that 'When inward transportation of input or capital goods is specifically covered by the definition of "input services", the insurance during inward of the transport of capital goods would also be covered.' It applied this rule to grant Cenvat credit for marine inland transit insurance of machinery transported from the supplier to the appellant. The Tribunal further held that 'service of insurance of money in transit is covered by the definition of "Input Service"' because such insurance is part of normal business activity for day-to-day cash operations. Concerning personnel insurance, the Tribunal accepted the appellant's position that 'the principal employer has vicarious liability for any compensation in the case of injury etc. to even contract workers' and relied on Millipore to treat medical and personnel accident policies as input services. The Tribunal qualified the scope by stating that services must be 'in or in relation to manufacturing business of the Assessee' and therefore denied credit for telephone services where the appellant 'have not shown as to how these services are in relation to his manufacturing business' and which 'may be substantially for non-business purposes also.'
final_order: The Tribunal set aside the impugned order except the portion upholding denial of Cenvat credit for telephone services installed at the General Manager's residence and the company's guest house; the denial of credit in respect of marine inland transit insurance, money-in-transit insurance, follow-up installation services and insurance of personnel, and the penalty, were set aside. The appeal was disposed of accordingly.
summary: This is a 2009 CESTAT appeal by Monnet Ispat challenging denial of Cenvat (service tax) credit on various insurance and installation-related services and a penalty. The tribunal held that transit insurance for inward transportation of capital goods, money-in-transit insurance, follow-up installation services and personnel accident insurance qualify as 'input services' and are eligible for Cenvat credit. The tribunal upheld denial only for telephone services at the GM's residence and guest house because their business nexus was not established. The rest of the demand and penalty were set aside.
embedding_summary: Monnet Ispat & Energy Ltd v. C.C.E., Raipur, CESTAT Court-IV, 22.12.2009, Central Excise Appeal No.142 of 2008-SM. Appeal against Commissioner (Appeals) order denying Cenvat credit (show cause dated 3.11.2006) of Rs.4,36,650 and confirming disallowance of Rs.38,510 (including marine inland transit insurance Rs.23,460), money-in-transit insurance, personnel accident insurance, follow-up services for installation of captive power plant, and certain telephone services; penalty Rs.25,000 under Rule 15(1) Cenvat Credit Rules, 2004. Key legal issues: definition of 'input service' under Cenvat Credit Rules, whether inward transportation/transit insurance of capital goods is admissible, whether money-in-transit and personnel (including contract workers) insurance are input services, and business nexus for telephone services. Tribunal relied on C.C.E., Mumbai v. GTC Industries Ltd. (Tri-LB), Finolex Cables Ltd. (Tri-Mumbai) 2009, Millipore India Ltd. (Tri-Bang.) 2009 and Board Circular No.665/56/2002-CX to hold transit insurance, money-in-transit insurance, follow-up installation services and personnel insurance eligible for Cenvat credit while telephone services were rightly denied. Outcome: majority of disallowances and penalty set aside; only telephone-service denial at GM residence and guest house upheld.
legal_principles: Transit insurance for inward transportation of capital goods is an 'input service' and eligible for Cenvat credit
Insurance for money in transit (factory to bank and back) is an 'input service' and eligible for Cenvat credit
Insurance of personnel (including contract workers) can be treated as an 'input service' because the principal employer has vicarious liability for compensation on injury
Services relating to setting up or modernisation (follow-up services for installation of plant & machinery) are input services eligible for credit

Services must be 'in or in relation to manufacturing business' to qualify; services substantially for non-business purposes (e.g., telephones at GM residence/guest house without demonstrated nexus) are not eligible
Board Circular No.665/56/2002-CX supports cenvat credit for capital goods used captively in manufacture even if final product or intermediate is exempt
cases_cited: C.C.E., Mumbai v. GTC Industries Ltd., 2008 (12) STR 468 (Tri-LB)
Finolex Cables Ltd. v. C.C.E., Pune I, 2009 (14) STR 303 (Tri-Mumbai)
Millipore India Ltd. v. C.C.E., Bangalore II, 2009 (236) ELT 145 (Tri-Bang.)

The appellant argued that transit insurance for plant and machinery, money-in-transit insurance, personnel accident insurance, follow-up services for installation and relevant telephone services constitute 'input services' and therefore qualify for Cenvat credit under the definition of input service; inward transportation of inputs/capital goods covers transit insurance. Counsel relied on Tribunal precedents (C.C.E., Mumbai v. GTC Industries Ltd. (Tri-LB), Finolex Cables Ltd., Millipore India Ltd.) and Board Circular No.665/56/2002-CX to show cenvat credit for capital goods and related services used in manufacturing even when electricity is for captive use. The Revenue contended that the services must be used in the factory in relation to manufacture of finished products to qualify; marine inland transit insurance, money-in-transit insurance, follow-up services and the telephone services at the GM's residence and guest house were not shown to be in relation to manufacture. The Tribunal held that 'When inward transportation of input or capital goods is specifically covered by the definition of "input services", the insurance during inward of the transport of capital goods would also be covered.' The Tribunal further held that 'service of insurance of money in transit is covered by the definition of "Input Service"' because such insurance is part of normal business activity for day-to-day cash operations. Concerning personnel insurance, the Tribunal accepted the appellant's position that 'the principal employer has vicarious liability for any compensation in the case of injury etc. to even contract workers' and relied on Millipore to treat medical and personnel accident policies as input services. The Tribunal set aside the impugned order except the portion upholding denial of Cenvat credit for telephone services installed at the General Manager's residence and the company's guest house; the denial of credit in respect of marine inland transit insurance, money-in-transit insurance, follow-up installation services and insurance of personnel, and the penalty, were set aside. The appeal was disposed of accordingly. This is a 2009 CESTAT appeal by Monnet Ispat challenging denial of Cenvat (service tax) credit on various insurance and installation-related services and a penalty. The tribunal held that transit insurance for inward transportation of capital goods, money-in-transit insurance, follow-up installation services and personnel accident insurance qualify as 'input services' and are eligible for Cenvat credit. The tribunal upheld denial only for telephone services at the GM's residence and guest house because their business nexus was not established. The rest of the demand and penalty were set aside. Monnet Ispat & Energy Ltd v. C.C.E., Raipur, CESTAT Court-IV, 22.12.2009, Central Excise Appeal No.142 of 2008-SM. Appeal against Commissioner (Appeals) order denying Cenvat credit (show cause dated 3.11.2006) of Rs.4,36,650 and confirming disallowance of Rs.38,510 (including marine inland transit insurance Rs.23,460), money-in-transit insurance, personnel accident insurance, follow-up services for installation of captive power plant, and certain telephone services; penalty Rs.25,000 under Rule 15(1) Cenvat Credit Rules, 2004. Key legal issues: definition of 'input service' under Cenvat Credit Rules, whether inward transportation/transit insurance of capital goods is admissible, whether money-in-transit and personnel (including contract workers) insurance are input services, and business nexus for telephone services. Tribunal relied on C.C.E., Mumbai v. GTC Industries Ltd. (Tri-LB), Finolex Cables Ltd. (Tri-Mumbai) 2009, Millipore India Ltd. (Tri-Bang.) 2009 and Board Circular No.665/56/2002-CX to hold transit insurance, money-in-transit insurance, follow-up installation services and personnel insurance eligible for Cenvat credit while telephone services were rightly denied. Outcome: majority of disallowances and penalty set aside; only telephone-service denial at GM residence and guest house upheld. Transit insurance for inward transportation of capital goods is an 'input service' and eligible for Cenvat credit Insurance for money in transit (factory to bank and back) is an 'input service' and eligible for Cenvat credit Insurance of personnel (including contract workers) can be treated as an 'input service' because the principal employer has vicarious liability for compensation on injury Services relating to setting up or modernisation (follow-up services for installation of plant & machinery) are input services eligible for credit Services must be 'in or in relation to manufacturing business' to qualify; services substantially for non-business purposes (e.g., telephones at GM residence/guest house without demonstrated nexus) are not eligible Board Circular No.665/56/2002-CX supports cenvat credit for capital goods used captively in manufacture even if final product or intermediate is exempt Rule 15(1) Cenvat Credit Rules, 2004 Cenvat Credit Rules, 2004 (definition of 'input service') Board Circular No.665/56/2002-CX dated 25th September 2002 C.C.E., Mumbai v. GTC Industries Ltd., 2008 (12) STR 468 (Tri-LB) Finolex Cables Ltd. v. C.C.E., Pune I, 2009 (14) STR 303 (Tri-Mumbai) Millipore India Ltd. v. C.C.E., Bangalore II, 2009 (236) ELT 145 (Tri-Bang.)
